 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-d0g","title":"Implement Dicklesworthstone stack updates (checksum-aware)","description":"## What
Implement the 'stack' category for acfs-update with SECURE checksum verification.

Tools to update:
- NTM (Named Tmux Manager)
- MCP Agent Mail
- UBS (Ultimate Bug Scanner)
- BV (Beads Viewer)
- CASS (Coding Agent Session Search)
- CM (CASS Memory System)
- CAAM (Coding Agent Account Manager)
- SLB (Simultaneous Launch Button)
- BD (Beads CLI) - the task tracker itself

## SECURITY: Checksum-Aware Update Flow
Unlike the "bypass checksums" approach, we MUST verify checksums to prevent executing compromised scripts:

1. Check if tool is installed (binary exists)
2. If not installed: skip (acfs-update doesn't install new tools)
3. If installed:
   a. Fetch installer script from GitHub
   b. Calculate SHA256 checksum
   c. Compare to checksums.yaml:
      - MATCH: Run installer safely (known-good script)
      - MISMATCH: Script changed upstream. Options:
        * Show warning with URL to review changes
        * If --yes: proceed (user accepts risk)
        * If interactive: prompt [S]kip / [P]roceed / [V]iew diff
        * Default: Skip and continue with other tools

## Why This Matters
Bypassing checksums would allow an attacker who compromises a GitHub repo to execute arbitrary code on all users who run `acfs-update`. The tradeoff of prompting on upstream changes is worth the security.

## Special Cases
- MCP Agent Mail: git pull + uv sync in ~/mcp_agent_mail (no installer re-run)
- BD (Beads): Check where installed, use appropriate update method

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All 9 stack tools update correctly
- [ ] Checksum verification works
- [ ] User prompted on checksum mismatch (unless --yes)
- [ ] MCP Agent Mail git+uv flow works
- [ ] Skips uninstalled tools
- [ ] Version changes logged","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:32:07.801083-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:13:32.430411-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:13:32.430411-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented in scripts/lib/update.sh:update_stack() with checksum verification via update_run_verified_installer for all 8 stack tools","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-d0g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:32:19.582864-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-db0","title":"Implement shell tool updates (omz, p10k, plugins, atuin, zoxide)","description":"## What
Implement the 'shell' category for acfs-update:
- Oh-My-Zsh: Run $ZSH/tools/upgrade.sh (non-interactive)
- Powerlevel10k: git pull --ff-only in $ZSH_CUSTOM/themes/powerlevel10k
- zsh-autosuggestions: git pull --ff-only in plugin dir
- zsh-syntax-highlighting: git pull --ff-only in plugin dir
- Atuin: Use atuin self-update if available, else reinstall
- Zoxide: Reinstall via official installer

## Git-Based Updates (omz, p10k, plugins)
- Use git pull --ff-only to avoid merge conflicts
- If fast-forward fails: warn and skip (user has local changes)
- OMZ has its own upgrade script that handles prompts

## Installer-Based Updates (Atuin, Zoxide)
These use curl|sh installers like the stack tools:
1. Atuin: Try `atuin self-update` first (if available)
   - If no self-update: fetch installer, verify checksum, run
2. Zoxide: Fetch installer, verify checksum against checksums.yaml, run

For checksum mismatches: same flow as stack tools (warn, prompt, skip/proceed)

## Considerations
- ZSH_CUSTOM defaults to $ZSH/custom if not set
- DISABLE_UPDATE_PROMPT=true for OMZ non-interactive
- Some users may have customized themes/plugins - respect that

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All shell tools update without prompts (in --yes mode)
- [ ] Git-based updates handle conflicts gracefully
- [ ] Atuin/Zoxide checksum verification works
- [ ] Before/after versions logged
- [ ] Skips missing components","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:26:10.463809-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:18:10.575757-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:18:10.575757-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented shell tool updates: omz, p10k, zsh plugins, atuin, zoxide","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-db0","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:25.018434-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

Establish real-data, no-mock unit coverage and complete E2E integration scripts with detailed, human-readable logging.

# Why
- Current tests are partial (installer E2E + web Playwright). No full unit coverage across core Bash libs or manifest tooling.
- E2E logs exist but are not standardized or granular enough for fast diagnosis.

# Success Criteria
- Unit coverage exists for manifest parser/generator, selection resolver, contract/security/preflight libs with real fixtures (no mocks/fakes).
- E2E scripts cover bootstrap + selection + resume flows with structured logs and artifacts.
- Tests are deterministic, diffable, and runnable locally + in CI.

# Notes
- "No mocks/fakes" means: use real manifests, real generated scripts, real flags; avoid stubbing core behaviors.
- Logging should be timestamped, sectioned, and include command echo + exit codes.

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fed.3","title":"Create manifest-to-installer generator","description":"# Task: Manifest-to-Installer Generator

## Description
Generate installer module scripts from acfs.manifest.yaml.

## Purpose
The manifest is the single source of truth. The installer shouldn't
duplicate this information - it should be generated.

## What It Generates

### For each module category, generate:
```bash
# scripts/generated/install_<category>.sh
# Auto-generated from acfs.manifest.yaml - DO NOT EDIT

install_base() {
    log_step "Installing base.curl"
    sudo apt-get install -y curl
    verify "curl --version"
    
    log_step "Installing base.git"
    # ... etc
}
```

### For doctor checks:
```bash
# scripts/generated/doctor_checks.sh
CHECKS=(
    "identity.user_is_ubuntu|Logged in as ubuntu|[[ \$(whoami) == 'ubuntu' ]]"
    # ... etc
)
```

## Implementation
- packages/manifest/generate.ts
- Reads acfs.manifest.yaml
- Outputs to scripts/generated/

## CI Integration
- Run generator on manifest changes
- Fail if generated files differ (ensure they're committed)

- [ ] Generator script works

- [ ] Doctor checks generated

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf","title":"EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting","description":"# Epic: Per-Phase Error Reporting

## Overview
Replace generic 'installation failed' with structured error reports showing exactly which phase/step failed and how to fix it.

## Problem Statement
When installation fails, users see only 'ACFS installation failed' with no indication of which phase (1-10), what step, what the error was, or how to fix it.

## Business Impact
- Users can self-diagnose (reduces support burden)
- 'Phase 6' is actionable; 'failed' is not
- Most failures have known fixes (network, rate limits, deps)

## Technical Approach
1. Add CURRENT_PHASE, CURRENT_STEP, LAST_ERROR globals
2. Create try_step() wrapper for individual operations
3. Build ERROR_PATTERNS map with known fixes
4. Create report_failure() with structured output
5. Log JSON failure report for programmatic use

## Success Criteria
- Failure reports show phase number, step name, error excerpt
- Suggested fixes shown for common error patterns
- Full log path and resume command included
- JSON structured log for tooling

## Dependencies
- Depends on: EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence (for state tracking)

## Files to Modify
- install.sh: Error tracking, try_step wrapper, report_failure
- scripts/lib/errors.sh (new): Error patterns database

-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hao","title":"[codex.3] Document OpenAI account types distinction","description":"## Goal
Add clear documentation explaining the difference between OpenAI API accounts and ChatGPT accounts.

## Why This Matters
Many users are confused because OpenAI has TWO distinct account/auth systems:

### 1. OpenAI API Account
- **Purpose**: Programmatic API access (for developers building apps)
- **Auth**: OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable
- **Billing**: Pay-per-token, separate API billing
- **Where**: platform.openai.com
- **What uses it**: OpenAI SDK, custom applications
- **NOT used by**: codex-cli

### 2. ChatGPT Account (Pro/Plus/Teams)
- **Purpose**: Consumer chat interface and coding tools
- **Auth**: OAuth via web browser (email/password or SSO)
- **Billing**: Monthly subscription
- **Where**: chat.openai.com
- **What uses it**: ChatGPT website, codex-cli, mobile app
- **Used by**: codex-cli!

## Documentation to Create/Update
1. Add section to README.md or create docs/agent-auth.md
2. Update acfs/onboard/lessons/04_agents_login.md with clarification
3. Consider adding to wizard website (apps/web/)

## Key Points to Document
1. codex-cli uses ChatGPT account, NOT API key
2. No OPENAI_API_KEY needed for codex-cli
3. Authentication flow: run "codex auth" → browser opens → login → done
4. May need to enable device access in ChatGPT settings
5. caam can backup/restore codex credentials just like claude/gemini

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Clear explanation of the two account types
2. Explicit statement that codex-cli uses ChatGPT, not API
3. Step-by-step auth guide for codex-cli
4.  {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-mjt.5.2","title":"Implement curl|bash archive bootstrap (atomic + validated + coherent)","description":"## Goal
Make `curl|bash` installs self-consistent, atomic, and debuggable by bootstrapping a **single repo archive** for a single ref.

## Why this matters (user impact)
- Mixed-ref installs are a real foot-gun: `install.sh` from commit A + generated scripts from commit B = undefined behavior.
- Piecemeal raw-file downloads increase the chance of partial downloads and truncation.
- Beginners need reliability: if something fails, we should fail early with a clear explanation.

## Requirements
### 1) Single-archive bootstrap
- Fetch `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/archive/<ref>.tar.gz` for a single `ACFS_REF`.
- Extract only the allowlisted runtime subset (final allowlist comes from `mjt.2.3`):
  - `scripts/lib/**`
  - `scripts/generated/**`
  - `scripts/preflight.sh` (Phase 0 preflight)
  - `acfs/**`
  - `checksums.yaml`
  - `acfs.manifest.yaml`

### 2) Atomic + validated
- Download to a temp file.
- Validate tarball exists and extraction succeeds.
- Run `bash -n` on extracted shell scripts before sourcing.
- Only after validation: point `ACFS_LIB_DIR/ACFS_GENERATED_DIR/...` at the extracted tree.

### 3) Coherence check (prevents mixed refs)
- Compute `sha256(acfs.manifest.yaml)`.
- Verify it matches `ACFS_MANIFEST_SHA256` in `scripts/generated/manifest_index.sh` (and/or generated headers).
- If mismatch: abort with a clear “bootstrap mismatch” error.

### 4) UX considerations
- Error messages must tell the user what to do next:
  - “Pin to a tag/sha” suggestion
  - “Retry” suggestion for transient failures
- Prefer using the shared curl wrapper (and its retry/backoff behavior) rather than raw `curl`.

### 5) Optional caching
- Optional: mirror extracted bootstrap under `$ACFS_HOME/cache/bootstrap/<ref>/` for faster re-runs.
- Must not break correctness (cache must be keyed by ref + validated).

## Acceptance criteria
- No process substitution sourcing.
- Never sources partially downloaded files.
- Clear failure messages for:
  - network errors
  - extraction errors
  - bash -n failures
  - manifest/generated mismatch

-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-mjt.5.4","title":"Implement acfs_resolve_selection (deps closure + skip safety + print-plan)","description":"## Goal
Compute the effective execution plan once (deterministic), then make per-module checks cheap.

## Inputs
- `scripts/generated/manifest_index.sh` (phase/deps/func/category/tags/defaults)
- CLI-derived selection sets:
  - `ONLY_*` (modules, phases; optionally tags/categories)
  - `SKIP_*` (modules; optionally tags/categories)
  - legacy flag shims (applied by mapping into SKIP/ONLY sets)
- Control flags:
  - `NO_DEPS` / `--no-deps`
  - `PRINT_PLAN` / `--print-plan`

## Algorithm (must be explicit + testable)
1) **Start set**
- If `--only` provided: start set = ONLY_MODULES
- Else if `--only-phase`: start set = all modules in those phases
- Else: start set = `enabled_by_default` modules

2) **Apply skips**
- Remove any `SKIP_MODULES`.
- (Optional if we choose): expand `SKIP_TAGS` / `SKIP_CATEGORIES` into concrete module IDs using manifest_index maps.

3) **Dependency closure (default)**
- Unless `--no-deps`, expand the start set by adding all transitive deps using `ACFS_MODULE_DEPS`.
- `--no-deps` MUST print a prominent warning.

4) **Skip safety**
- If a required dependency is skipped, fail early with an actionable message:
  - show the module, the missing dep, and at least one dependency chain.

5) **Derive deterministic ordered plan**
- `ACFS_EFFECTIVE_PLAN=(...)` should be computed by filtering `ACFS_MODULES_IN_ORDER`.
- `should_run_module` becomes a cheap membership test (`ACFS_EFFECTIVE_RUN[module]=1`).

6) **Plan/introspection outputs**
- `--print-plan` prints the deterministic plan and exits (no state mutation).
- `--list-modules` lists available modules using manifest_index data (never hard-coded).

## Error semantics
- Unknown module id (in `--only`/`--skip`) => hard error.
- Unknown phase => hard error.
- `--skip` that breaks deps => hard error.

## Acceptance criteria
- Behavior matches the spec examples in `mjt.1.2`.
- Output is stable/diffable and does not mutate state.

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-mjt.5.5","title":"Preserve legacy flags by mapping to tags/modules","description":"## Goal
Keep wizard and docs working while changing internal implementation.

## Why this matters (user impact)
If we break existing flags, beginners will follow the wizard/README and hit confusing behavior drift (“the docs say X but the installer did Y”). This task ensures compatibility while still allowing the manifest-driven engine to be the only real source of behavior.

## Requirements
### 1) Legacy flags become pure selection config
Existing flags (example set; confirm via inventory):
- `--skip-postgres`
- `--skip-vault`
- `--skip-cloud`

Must be implemented as:
- adding module IDs to `SKIP_MODULES`, and/or
- adding tags/categories to `SKIP_TAGS` / `SKIP_CATEGORIES` (if we implement them)

### 2) No special-case installation logic
- No `if [[ $SKIP_CLOUD == 1 ]]; then ... fi` inside install steps.
- The orchestrator should just execute the resolved plan; selection decides what’s included.

### 3) Prevent drift
- Prefer mapping to tags/categories derived from `manifest_index.sh` so the mapping stays in sync as modules evolve.
- At minimum: keep the mapping table documented in the plan doc and validated by tests.

## Acceptance criteria
- Running install with legacy flags produces the same behavior as before.
- Legacy flags are wrappers over the manifest selection engine.
- `--print-plan` shows legacy-derived skips as explicit exclusions.

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-mjt.5.6","title":"Add feature flags for incremental category rollout (generated vs legacy)","description":"## Goal
Make the migration safe and reversible without commented-out code.

## Why this matters
This refactor touches `install.sh` (high-risk). Feature flags let us roll out manifest-driven installs category-by-category while preserving a reliable rollback path.

## Requirements
- Provide a **global** toggle:
  - `ACFS_USE_GENERATED=0|1` (default 1 once migration begins)
- Provide **per-category** toggles so we can migrate incrementally:
  - e.g. `ACFS_USE_GENERATED_BASE`, `..._SHELL`, `..._LANG`, `..._AGENTS`, `..._CLOUD`, `..._STACK`, `..._ACFS`

Behavior:
- If per-category toggle is set, it overrides the global.
- If toggles are unset, default behavior is “use generated for migrated categories; legacy for unmigrated categories”.

## Guardrails
- The orchestrator remains the single owner of:
  - state/resume
  - `run_phase` framing
  - `try_step` / `report_failure`
- Feature flags must not bypass those wrappers; they only choose *which installer function* to invoke.

## Acceptance criteria
- A maintainer can flip one env var to force legacy behavior for a category (for debugging / rollback).
- No commented legacy code blocks; rollback paths remain executable.
- Plan/test coverage ensures both paths stay working during migration.

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-mjt.5.7","title":"Create scripts/lib/install_helpers.sh (run_as_*_shell + exec helpers)","description":"## Goal
Provide a small, well-tested installer helper library that generated modules can rely on.

## Why this matters
The generator should emit install/verify steps as multi-line scripts (heredocs) executed under the correct user context. If we don’t provide a standard run-as-shell abstraction, we’ll end up with ad-hoc `bash -lc ...` calls, inconsistent PATH behavior, and subtle quoting/pipefail bugs.

## Requirements
### 1) run-as shell helpers (heredoc-friendly)
- `run_as_target_shell` executes stdin (or a string) as `TARGET_USER`.
- `run_as_root_shell` executes stdin (or a string) as root.
- `run_as_current_shell` executes stdin (or a string) as the current user.

Each helper must:
- support multi-line scripts and pipes reliably
- enforce strict mode *inside the executed shell* (`set -euo pipefail`)
- preserve correct PATH (especially for target user installs)

### 2) command existence helpers
- `command_exists` (current user)
- `command_exists_as_target` (target user)

### 3) compatibility constraints
- Must compose with existing `run_as_target` logic (don’t break existing call sites).
- Must work when `TARGET_USER` is already the current user.
- Must not introduce side effects at source-time.

## Acceptance criteria
- Generated modules can execute both one-liner and heredoc install steps correctly.
- A multi-line heredoc with pipes fails correctly under strict mode.
- ShellCheck passes for the helper library.

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-qup","title":"Show skipped tools in acfs doctor output","description":"# Task: Show skipped tools in acfs doctor output

## Purpose
When doctor runs, clearly show which tools were intentionally skipped vs which failed unexpectedly.

## Critical Distinction: Intentional Skip vs Bug

**Problem:** Without this distinction, doctor output is confusing:
- User intentionally skipped `ohmyzsh` via checksum prompt
- Doctor shows: `[✗] ohmyzsh: not found`
- User thinks: "Did installation break?"

**Solution:** Track skip reasons and display appropriately.

### Skip Tracking in state.json
```json
{
  "skipped_tools": {
    "ohmyzsh": {
      "reason": "checksum_mismatch",
      "skipped_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",
      "user_choice": "skip"
    },
    "atuin": {
      "reason": "network_error",
      "skipped_at": "2025-01-15T10:32:00Z",
      "retries": 3
    }
  }
}
```

### Doctor Output Format
```
ACFS Doctor Report
==================

Installed Tools:
  [✓] mise 2024.12.0
  [✓] uv 0.5.0
  [✓] bun 1.2.0
  [✓] go 1.23.0
  ...

Intentionally Skipped:
  [○] ohmyzsh - skipped (checksum mismatch, user choice)
  [○] atuin - skipped (network error after 3 retries)

Missing (unexpected):
  [✗] lazygit - not found (should be installed by mise)
  [✗] starship - not found

Legend: [✓] installed  [○] skipped  [✗] missing
```

### Key Design Decisions

1. **Use `[○]` for skipped** - visually distinct from `[✓]` (success) and `[✗]` (error)
2. **Show skip reason** - user knows WHY it was skipped
3. **Separate section** - dont mix intentional skips with failures
4. **Missing = unexpected** - tools that SHOULD exist but dont

### Implementation
```bash
show_tool_status() {
    local tool="$1"
    local state_file="$ACFS_STATE_DIR/state.json"
    
    # Check if intentionally skipped
    if jq -e ".skipped_tools.\"$tool\"" "$state_file" &>/dev/null; then
        local reason=$(jq -r ".skipped_tools.\"$tool\".reason" "$state_file")
        echo "[○] $tool - skipped ($reason)"
        return
    fi
    
    # Check if installed
    if command -v "$tool" &>/dev/null; then
        local version=$("$tool" --version 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "installed")
        echo "[✓] $tool $version"
    else
        echo "[✗] $tool - not found"
    fi
}
```

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t9i","title":"EPIC: Enhanced Doctor with Functional Tests","description":"# Epic: Enhanced Doctor with Functional Tests

## Overview
Add --deep flag to acfs doctor that performs functional smoke tests, not just binary existence checks.

## Problem Statement
acfs doctor only checks if binaries exist, not if they work. It says 'All critical checks passed' but cc immediately fails with 'not authenticated'. Binary existence ≠ functional.

## Business Impact
- Auth issues are the most common post-install failure
- Users copy aliases but skip authentication
- Deep checks catch drift (tokens expire, configs corrupted)
- Reduces 'it says it passed but nothing works' support tickets

## Technical Approach
1. Add --deep flag (default doctor stays fast)
2. Agent auth checks: Claude config exists + test call, Codex OPENAI_API_KEY, Gemini credentials
3. Database checks: PostgreSQL connection and role verification
4. Cloud CLI checks: Vault configured, Wrangler/Supabase/Vercel auth status
5. Token expiration warnings where applicable
6. Cache results to avoid rate limiting

## Success Criteria
- --deep catches all auth configuration issues
- Each check has actionable fix suggestion
- 4 status levels: pass, warn, fail, info (skip)
- Maintains backwards compatibility (default doctor unchanged)

## Files to Modify
- scripts/lib/doctor.sh: Add --deep flag and functional tests

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-tx7","title":"EPIC: Checkpoint-Based Checksum Recovery","description":"# Epic: Checkpoint-Based Checksum Recovery

## Overview
Handle checksum mismatches gracefully by offering skip/continue options for non-critical tools instead of failing the entire installation.

## Problem Statement
If ANY of 14 upstream tools fails checksum verification, entire install fails. One stale checksum on tool #8 wastes all progress from tools 1-7.

## Business Impact
- Checksum mismatches are usually legitimate updates, not attacks
- Not all tools equally critical: missing ntm is annoying; missing bun is blocking
- Completed phases should not be wasted by tool #9 failing

## Technical Approach
1. Classify tools as CRITICAL (bun, rust, uv, claude) vs RECOMMENDED (ntm, bv, cass, etc.)
2. Critical mismatches: still abort (security-sensitive)
3. Recommended mismatches: offer interactive skip/abort/proceed options
4. Track skipped tools in state.json
5. Report skipped tools at install completion with manual install commands
6. Add --strict flag for current behavior (abort on ANY mismatch)

## Success Criteria
- Critical tool mismatch aborts with clear explanation
- Non-critical mismatch offers user choice
- Skipped tools tracked and reported
- Manual install commands provided for skipped tools

## Files to Modify
- scripts/lib/security.sh: Criticality classification, recovery logic
- install.sh: Use new recovery flow, track skipped tools
